coredata.dc:description: Leachate management in an efficient way is still a challenge as it contains many emerging pollutants. This work activates the persulfate by defatted Moringa oleifera seeds waste-derived graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs) catalyst to degrade the matured leachate pollutants by electro-oxidation with scrap stainless steel as electrodes. At the optimized conditions with pH of 3, 4.5 V, 1 g/L of persulfate, 0.07 g/L of M-GQDs, 3 h of reaction time, the removal of UV254, chemical oxygen demand (COD), total organic carbon (TOC), and NH3-N is found to be 99 %, 97 %, 84 %, and 90 % respectively with less energy consumption of 0.44 kWh/kg COD. The 3D fluorescence excitation-emission spectrophotometer (3D EEM) and the Gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS) analysis results attune the degradation of dissolved organic matter and micropollutants in the leachate. From the above-mentioned electro-oxidation treatment generated sludge, the quantum dots (S-CQDs), have been synthesized and characterized. Both the M-GQDs and S-CQDs are green emissive, spherical, and hydrophilic, with an average particle size of 2 to 4 nm, a band gap of 4 eV, and a quantum yield of 3.91 %, & 6.73 % respectively. Both have shown good suitability for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting fingerprint enhancement without changing the latent shape. The work is innovative because it broadens the application of quantum dots to the purification of highly contaminated wastewater and the development of materials for industry from generated waste for a closed-loop sustainability system.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots/persulfate boosted electro-oxidation for leachate degradation, carbon quantum dots extraction for anti-counterfeiting applications - a sustainable approach

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental and Water Resources Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632 014, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental and Water Resources Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental and Water Resources Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore-632 014 Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Leachate management in an efficient way is still a challenge as it contains many emerging pollutants. This work activates the persulfate by defatted Moringa oleifera seeds waste-derived graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs) catalyst to degrade the matured leachate pollutants by electro-oxidation with scrap stainless steel as electrodes. At the optimized conditions with pH of 3, 4.5 V, 1 g/L of persulfate, 0.07 g/L of M-GQDs, 3 h of reaction time, the removal of UV254, chemical oxygen demand (COD), total organic carbon (TOC), and NH3-N is found to be 99 %, 97 %, 84 %, and 90 % respectively with less energy consumption of 0.44 kWh/kg COD. The 3D fluorescence excitation-emission spectrophotometer (3D EEM) and the Gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS) analysis results attune the degradation of dissolved organic matter and micropollutants in the leachate. From the above-mentioned electro-oxidation treatment generated sludge, the quantum dots (S-CQDs), have been synthesized and characterized. Both the M-GQDs and S-CQDs are green emissive, spherical, and hydrophilic, with an average particle size of 2 to 4 nm, a band gap of 4 eV, and a quantum yield of 3.91 %, & 6.73 % respectively. Both have shown good suitability for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting fingerprint enhancement without changing the latent shape. The work is innovative because it broadens the application of quantum dots to the purification of highly contaminated wastewater and the development of materials for industry from generated waste for a closed-loop sustainability system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to urbanization, globalization, population increase, and the cultural transformations toward consumerism, the municipal waste generation rate has increased considerably [1]. In many nations, landfills are the most common way to dispose of solid waste due to their low cost and convenience [2]. The leachate formed by landfills contains a wide spectrum of organic and inorganic compounds, ammoniacal nitrogen, heavy metals, etc., [3]. It also comprises toxins, carcinogens like pharmaceutical and personal care products, microplastics, endocrine disruptors [4], and liquid crystal monomers [5]. It also has many undetected contaminants [6].

Several physicochemical and biological methods have been suggested for the treatment of leachate. The advanced oxidation technique is one of the most successful techniques for breaking down the leachate contaminants [7]. Different types of advanced oxidation methods such as photo-Fenton, electrochemical oxidation, Fenton's oxidation, ozonation, peroxone, and photolysis/photocatalysis have been extensively investigated for treating leachate pollutants. However, several of these treatments have substantial downsides, such as high operational costs, the formation of iron sludges, H2O2 storage concerns, ozone losses to the atmosphere, and the difficult removal or recovery of heterogeneous catalysts [8].

The electrochemical-aided advanced oxidation method has captured the interest of researchers for the past few years due to its simplicity and efficiency in treating wastewater with less time consumption [9]. Different oxidation methodologies, including the electro-Fenton process, electrochemical reduction, electro-coagulation, three-dimensional electrode process, ion exchange membrane, electro-ozonation, biological electrochemical processes, etc., have been reported with and without the additions of catalysts [10]. When the persulfate salts are oxidized, they produce sulfate radicals with a higher oxidation potential (Eo = 2.5–3.1 V) than OH• radicals. (E0 = 1.8–2.7 V) which are more advantageous than many other wastewater treatment methods [11]. The production of persulfate radicals can be triggered in multiple ways like by heat, sonication, ultraviolet rays, microwaves, ozone, electricity, transitional metal, and carbonaceous materials [12].

Particularly, the carbon-based sulfate radicals activation is highly preferable due to its low cost, ease of availability, recyclability, eco-friendliness, high surface area, and stability for a wide pH range [13,14]. The king of the elements, carbon, is profound and promising in the use of numerous cutting-edge technologies [15]. Many forms of carbon have been used for the activation of persulfate, like graphene-based materials, porous carbons, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanobubbles, carbon aerogels, carbon microspheres, quantum dots, etc., with and without various dopants [14].

The CQDs (carbon quantum dots) are a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with the properties of facile synthesis, low toxicity, strong biocompatibility, and great photostability [16]. The CQDs are numerous, affordable, possess unique electron-transfer properties, and a large specific surface area, all of which perform well for the electrochemical reactions [17]. Also, the CQDs, as a type of crucial component, could be easily coupled with other compounds to increase their catalytic characteristics due to the abundance of functional groups on the surface [18]. Furthermore, the CQDs function incredibly well as a co-catalyst in water molecule splitting reactions [15].

The field of research focused on the electro-oxidation of persulfate with carbon catalysis is gaining prominence due to the aforementioned advantages. However, the investigations, particularly those focusing on the degradation of real-time wastewater using quantum dots as a catalyst, are quite limited. For instance, the persulfate-aided with carbon quantum dots catalysts has been investigated for single pollutant removals like bisphenol-F [19], tetracycline [20], and chromium VI [21], reduction in synthetic solutions. Hence, there is a huge research gap in investigating the various types of CQDs for the degradation of different pollutants with the aid of persulfate tandem electro-oxidation.

In this study, the stabilized municipal leachate has been treated by the electro-oxidation method boosted with persulfate and biowaste-defatted Moringa oleifera seeds-derived graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs) as catalysts. The M-GQDs are synthesized from defatted Moringa seeds which are considered to be bio-waste from the oil industry. The Moringa tree is often denoted as a “miracle tree” because it can be used in many ways, including as a food addition, animal feed, fencing, firewood, gum coagulant to clean water, and many medicinal values. The oil extracted from the seeds is widely used for food, cosmetics, medicine, and biodiesel in diverse regions of the globe [22]. After the extraction of oil, the residual solid material derived from the seeds is commonly referred to as “cake” [23]. According to recent studies, this raw cake has many potential uses, especially in the treatment of wastewater. These uses include its use as a chemical to remove pharmaceutical waste from the water, a biological absorbent to remove lead and chromium from the water source, and a bio-coagulant in wastewater treatment [24].

It is usually possible to synthesize quantum dots through both top-down and bottom-up methods [25]. The investigation of simple, inexpensive, and ecologically friendly methods for synthesizing quantum dots is essential for their widespread commercialization [26]. In the current study, we synthesized quantum dots from the oil-extracted Moringa seed cake using a bottom-up hydrothermal method.

To achieve efficient electro-oxidation, the utilization of electrodes such as boron-doped diamond [27], platinum [28], and rhenium [29] may be required. However, the practicality of employing these electrodes for economical operation may be questionable. Numerous studies have been dedicated to the exploration of alternative materials capable of substituting costly electrodes [30]. Stainless steel is employed in this investigation as electrodes recovered from construction debris. Stainless steel possesses the characteristic of being entirely recyclable, rendering it a viable and sustainable choice as electrodes [31].

This paper presents a study that aims to investigate the potential of combining electro-oxidation, with green-synthesized M-GQDs and persulfate as catalysts to treat the stabilized leachate. The analysis of various parameters confirmed the degradation of organics. The quantum dots are also synthesized from the sludge (S-CQDs) generated in the electro-oxidation treatment of leachate and characterized. Both the dots M-GQDs and S-CQDs give good green fluorescence under UV light. It has been successfully applied in fingerprint development as a green anti-counterfeiting material. Thus, we are introducing a new concept of WWW (Wastewater treatment with Waste generated materials and simultaneous conversion of Waste into commercial products) similar to RRR (Reduce, Reuse, and Recycle) for a sustainable environment.

The leachate has been collected from a closed landfill on the outskirts of Vellore, Tamil Nadu, India. Large piles of the city's municipal solid garbage have been dumped here for the past 50 years. Currently, it has been closed and is not in operation. But the previously dumped waste is generating leachate. It has channels for the transportation of leachate generated from the dump and is getting collected in the larger collection pits. The leachate is extracted from the site, relocated swiftly, and stored in a laboratory at or below 4 °C for further research. The characterization of raw leachate is given in Table ST1.

All the chemicals used in this study like sodium hydroxide (98 %), sulfuric acid (98 % w/w), ethanol (>99 %), tertiary butanol (>99 %), and sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8, 98 %), are of analytical grade and purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Private Limited, Bangalore, India. The defatted cakes of Moringa oleifera seeds were bought from a store in Coimbatore that extracts oil using the cold press method. Millipore's MilliQ Direct-Ultrapure Water System deionized water is utilized for the experiments. Two plates made of stainless steel type 304, standardized by ISO 3506, are utilized as electrodes. Himedia Dialysis membrane-70 with a cut-off of 500 Da to 1000 Da is used for the dialyzing of quantum dots.

A UV–Vis spectrophotometer is used to do the UV–Vis (Hach USA DR6000). The DC power supply is a GW INSTEK GPS-4303 type. XRD analysis is performed using the Bruker D8 Advance model. Shimadzu's TOC-L series instrument is utilized to measure total organic carbon (TOC), total inorganic carbon (TIC), and total carbon (TC). The conductivity is quantified using a Systronics MK-509 digital conductivity meter. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis is carried out with Quantachrome Autosorb iQc at 77 K liquid nitrogen temperature. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis is done using the instrument Model IR Affinity-1, Shimadzu. For Raman investigation is carried out at the excitation wavelength of 532 nm, using the HORIBA, LabRam HR Evolution model. The nanoscale images of M-GQDs are captured through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of model JEOL 3010. The particle size analysis from the HRTEM image is processed using IMAGEJ software. The 3D excitation-emission matrix (EEM) is examined using the Fluorescence Spectrophotometer of instrument Hitachi F7000 with a scanning speed of 12,000 nm/min, an applied voltage of 450 V, a scanning interval of 5 nm, a slit width of 5 nm, a xenon arc lamp with 150 W, a quartz cuvette of 1 cm pathlength and a wavelength confinement of 200 to 600 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis is also done using this instrument. The thermal stability of the powder quantum dots is analyzed using the Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) on the model TA Instruments, SDT Q600. The TGA is run at 50 °C to 800 °C with a nitrogen gas sparge rate of 100 mL/min and a steady temperature rise of 20 °C/min. Using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Avio 200), the metal analysis is carried out. Using ULVAC-PHI equipment of PHI5000 Version, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to analyze the elemental composition and structural purity. The electrokinetic surface charge potential of M-GQDs is determined by using a zeta potential analyzer of model Horiba Scientific SZ 100. A 7890 A GC system performed the gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis coupled with a Mass Selective Detector (MSD) 5977B (Agilent Technologies) run by a software package, namely Agilent Chem-Station. The GC has an Agilent HP-5MS non-polar capillary column of 30 m in length, internal diameter of 0.25 mm, thickness of the film of 0.25 μm as the stationary phase, and helium as the carrier gas, with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. About 1.0 μL of the sample is injected into the GC–MS with the oven temperature at 40 °C, then, at a rate of 5 °C per min, it is increased to 250 °C with a hold time of 3 min. The injector is held at 300 °C in ionization mode at 70 eV with a scan range of 40 to 650 mass-to-charge (m/z) ratio. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) database (2008) library is utilized to identify the chemicals. Biological oxygen demand (BOD) (5210 B), total solids (2540 A), Chemical oxygen demand (COD) (5220C), chlorides (4500 B), and ammoniacal nitrogen (NH3−N) (4500 F) determinations have been accomplished by APHA [32]. After passing the sample through a 0.22 μm filter, the amount of dissolved organic carbon (DOC) is determined by the TOC analysis. To determine the specific ultraviolet absorbance (SUVA) in units of L/mg-M, divide the sample's UV absorbance (in cm−1) by its DOC (in mg/L) and multiply by 100 cm/M.

The defatted Moringa seeds have been transformed into M-GQDs using the hydrothermal technique [33]. The defatted Moringa seeds are dried in a hot air oven until all the moisture disappears and crushed using a domestic mixer grinder. The powder is sieved in a 0.15 mm sieve to obtain a fine and smooth powder. After adding 30 mL of double-distilled water to the autoclave Teflon tube containing the sieved powder (5 g), the mixture is heated to 180 °C for 11 h in a hot air oven. After that, it is allowed to cool to ambient temperature, and a 0.22 μm PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) membrane filter is used to filter the dark brown solution inside the Teflon tube. This filtrate is subjected to dialysis for about 24 h for further purification. The liquid left inside the dialysis tube contains pure quantum dots, which are freeze-dried (lyophilized) to obtain powder M-GQDs. This synthesizing pathway is detailed in Fig. 1 . The generated liquid and solid M-GQDs are kept in glass vials and then preserved in a refrigerator under 4 °C.

The M-GQDs catalytic electro-persulfate oxidation investigations are carried out in a glass beaker of 1 L capacity, with 800 mL of leachate filled for every single experiment. For examination, two rectangular stainless steel plates measuring 21 cm by 2.4 cm by 0.5 cm, one of which is the cathode and the other the anode, with a total optimal contact surface of 16.8 cm2, has been submerged in the leachate. Aeration of atmospheric air has been facilitated adjacent to the cathode vicinity. The configuration of the reactor with the overall mechanism of the electro-oxidation reaction is given in Fig. 2 . The initial pH modification of leachate is done with 5 N NaOH or 5 N H2SO4. Then, the measured quantity of sodium persulfate salt is added and blended well in a magnetic stirrer for 2 min at 400 rpm. After 5 min, the catalyst M-GQDs powder is added and mixed with the use of a magnetic stirrer at 400 rpm for 5 min. After carefully blending, the aerator and the power supply are switched on simultaneously. Required leachate samples are fetched systematically for further investigation. All the trials are carried out at room temperature. The electrode plates are laved and dried immediately after every experiment to circumvent corrosion.

After the electro-oxidation experiments, the sludge that remained at the bottom of the reactor was utilized to prepare the quantum dots (S-CQDs). The S-CQDs are synthesized using a hydrothermal technique. After collecting the wet sludge, it is dried at 60 °C in a hot air oven until all of the moisture dissipates. A 100 mL Teflon autoclave reactor is filled with 5 g of dry material & 75 mL of MilliQ water and then sealed in a stainless steel autoclave. It is maintained at 150 °C for six hours in a hot air oven. It is then removed and allowed to cool to room temperature or 26 °C. To further purify the dark brown solution, a 0.22 μm PVDF membrane filter has been used, and the mixture is dialyzed for a whole day. To obtain quantum dots powder, the solution that is still on the dialysis membrane after 24 h is dried in a freezer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of M-GQDs and S-CQDs for anti-counterfeiting applications
ce:section.ce:para: The individual carefully washed and dried the hands before providing the fingerprints. The individual used the thumb finger to dab at the perspiration on the forehead, leaving a normal-force fingerprint impression on the plain white paper for fingerprint identification. Using a pestle and mortar, the dark powder of M-GQDs and S-CQDs is ground into a smooth powder that is then applied to the fingerprint using a smooth fingerprint brush. The extra powder is carefully removed using the brush tip [34]. After that, the image is captured under normal light and UV light at 365 nm. Fig. 9(a) depicts the technique for developing fingerprints.

The structural, optical, functional, and morphological properties of the synthesized M-GQDs from various analyses are discussed below. The morphology is analyzed using HRTEM analysis. The HRTEM image in Fig. 3(a) and supplementary Fig. S1(a) shows that the M-GQDs are tiny and spherical. The SAED pattern confirms the amorphous nature of carbon [35] as shown in Fig. 3(b), with a fringe width of 0.21 nm Fig. 3(c & d), demonstrating the graphitic carbon's presence. The structural defect showing the improper closing of rings is also clearly visible in Fig. 3(d). The quantum dots are randomly distributed and tend to aggregate soon, as shown in Fig. S1(b) owing to the absence of passivating agents. The M-GQDs have a typical particle size of 2.5 nm, and their distribution is established by the graphical Gaussian fits in Fig. S1(c). The formation of M-GQDs consists of two stages: The first phase is hydrothermal hydrolysis, which reduces the bigger organic molecules into smaller molecules. The next phase involves the conversion of smaller molecules to hydrothermal carbonization to generate M-GQDs [36]. The graphitic aromatic structures are formed by high pressure and temperature, as well as the production of H+ and OH− ions. Furthermore, OH• radicals play an important role in the cleavage of graphitic domains into GQDs [37].

The Raman spectra exhibit two large peaks, as shown in Fig. 3(e) corresponding to the crystalline G band and the disordered D band, at 1586 cm−1 and 1333 cm−1 representing the existence of disordered sp2 carbon in CC stretching mode and neatly ordered graphite, respectively [38]. The Raman spectrum's D peak is attributed to the “unorganized” carbon found around graphene's edges or flaws [39]. The characteristic bands of the GQDs have a relative intensity (ID/IG) of approximately 1.42, suggesting that there is more presence of graphitic structure and more crystal defects since the D band is stronger than the G band [40–43].

The zeta potential analysis is carried out to inspect the potential colloidal stability and surface charge of the M-GQDs. From the analysis, the zeta potential value is found to be −12.6 mV as given in Fig. 3(f). The considerably higher negative value indicates that the M-GQDs are well dispersed in the medium and have strong stability with the presence of anionic surface functional groups -OH and -COOH which is in concurrence with the previous literature [38,44].

The XRD pattern of M-GQDs is given in Fig. 4(a). The peak confirms that the material is amorphous. The large peak at 220, corresponding to graphite lattice spacing, implies disordered carbon atoms, consistent with the HRTEM data. [45]. The (002) plane (JCPDS Card No., 75–0444) causes GQDs to peak around 220, suggesting that the Moringa seeds have been transformed into M-GQDs [40], Fig. 4(b) shows the representation of the FT-IR pattern. The broad peak at 3270 cm−1 is due to the presence of the OH functional groups [46]. The CO stretching vibrations could be the cause of the tiny band at 1600 cm−1. The very small peak at 1400 cm−1 is due to the CN stretching vibrations. The CO and the CN stretching demonstrate the occurrence of the amide bonds on the quantum dots' surface [38]. The possible cause of the peak at 1109 cm−1 is the CO stretching of ethers and/or secondary alcohols [47]. The possible cause of the peak at 1109 cm−1 is the CO stretching of ethers and/or secondary alcohols [48]. The band at 713 cm−1 is owing to the presence of CH groups [49]. The band at 617 cm−1 is due to the presence of the CS stretching vibration of the hydrogen sulfide. The existence of these functional groups suggests that the generated M-GQDs are hydrophilic [50,51].

The TGA is assessed to check the thermal stability of the M-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The M-GQDs lose 6.478 % of their weight during the first phase at temperatures ranging from 50 °C to 350 °C. This is due to the loss of moisture in the sample [52]. It may be due to the hydrophilic functional groups' moisture absorption in the M-GQDs from the atmospheric air. The weight loss in the second phase between temperatures ranging from 350 °C to 800 °C is 22.74 %. This may be due to the oxidation of surface particles [53]. After heating to 800 °C, the residue remains at 73.74 %, establishing the good thermal stability of the M-GQDs [52].

Under UV light, the M-GQDs emit green fluorescence, as seen in Fig. 1. which agrees with the size of the quantum dots. Fig. 4(d) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. The peak of about 310 nm is caused by the n-π transition, when excited-state energy is trapped by surface states, resulting in strong emission. Additionally, it indicates the existence of a polycyclic aromatic CO bond transition, which is in line with the FT-IR findings [38,54]. The following formula yields the bandgap energy (E) of the produced M-GQDs. Where λ is the matching cutoff wavelength, C is the light velocity, and h is Planck's constant. The standard value of hc is taken as 1240 eV.nm [55]. The band gap energy is calculated to be 4 eV, which is well similar to the previous literature [16,56].
(1)
E =
hc λ

Fig. 4(e) displays the PL spectra at various excitation wavelengths with a 20 nm increase from 240 to 460 nm. The presence of surface defects and heteroatoms with distinct emissive sites in the quantum dots is indicated by decreased emission intensities and a red shift at excitation wavelengths >320 nm [54,57]. The PL analysis for variation of pH is shown in Fig. 4(f). There is no significant shift in wavelength when the pH is varied (acidic to basic), suggesting that the M-GQDs are independent of pH variation. However, there is a decrease in PL intensity when the MQDs are acidic and neutral, which is consistent with the prior observations. [58,59]. The excitation wavelength dependency PL emission of the quantum dots is due to the presence of many surface states and various functional groups. The quantum yield for the synthesized M-GQDs is estimated per the equation below (2).
(2)

where the quantum yield of M-GQDs is denoted by QYM-GQDs. The quantum yield of quinine sulfate, used as a reference, is given by Qref (0.54). The integrated fluorescence intensities of the sample and reference at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm are represented by IQD and Iref. The absorbance intensities of the reference and the sample are indicated by Aref and AQD. The refractive index of the sample and reference solution is denoted by ŋQD (distilled water, ŋ = 1.33) and ŋref (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent for quinine sulfate, ŋ = 1.33) [16]. The calculated quantum yield of 3.91 % is consistent with earlier research [60].

The XPS analysis is carried out to confirm surface functional group presence. As can be seen in Fig. 5(a), the complete survey scan of the XPS spectrum exhibits the major characteristic peaks of N 1s, C 1s, and O 1s. The deconvolution spectrum of the C 1s is shown in Fig. 5(b) and consists of three peaks at 288.1 eV, 286.1 eV, and 284.7 eV which may be due to the CO, CO, and CC bonds, respectively [61]. It is important to acknowledge that the presence of a distinct peak at about 284.7 eV is indicative of the existence of graphitic compounds [62]. The presence of CO and CO signals provides evidence for the presence of carbonyl and carboxyl functional groups in M-GQDs [54]. The N 1s spectra as shown in Fig. 5(c) of the M-GQDs exhibited prominent peaks at 400.60 eV, 399.9 eV, and 399.70 eV, which can be attributed to the interactions between carbon and nitrogen viz., pyrrole-like N (C–N–C) [38], pyridinic nitrogen (C-N-H) and amino functional groups (−NH), [63]. Additionally, the deconvolution of the O 1s spectrum as represented in Fig. 5(d), presented three summits at 532.3 eV, 531.9 eV, and 531.2 eV, which may be due to the presence of -O-C=O, [61] CO [64], and C − O bonds [62], respectively. Based on the XPS findings, the M-GQDs are confirmed to have hydrophilic functional groups on their surfaces which is per the previous literature [37].

The BET analysis shows that the M-GQDs contain 22.185 m2/g of surface area, volume of the pore as 0.034 cc/g, and diameter of the pore as 5.551 nm. The BET analysis results in plots of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms, and pore size distribution curves are given in Fig. S2 (a) and (b) [65]. The GC–MS analysis of Moringa powder and synthesized M-GQDs is performed to investigate the phytoconstituents of defatted seeds, their changes during hydrothermal treatment, and the presence of any harmful nature. A total of 175 compounds have been identified in the M-GQDs. Compounds like 9-Octadecenoic acid ethyl ester, trans-2-Dodecen-1-ol, trifluoroacetate, heptadecanoic acid, ethyl ester, etc., have been detected in major peaks which are non-toxic. Fig. S3 and Table ST2 in the supplementary show the raw curve with the compounds detected. The GC–MS data demonstrate that the M-GQDs catalyst produced is non-hazardous and safe for the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the electro-oxidation treatment of leachate

ce:section.ce:para:
It is commonly acknowledged that the value of pH can substantially impact the efficiency of electro-oxidation treatment [66]. It also influences the number of free radicals of chlorine and OH• generated in the reaction mixture [67]. Oxygen-reactive species, surface charges on carbon-based materials, and target pollutants' dissociation morphologies can all be impacted by the pH of the solution [12]. The raw leachate is dark brown with a COD of 9600 mg/L, and pH of 8.9, indicating that the leachate is alkaline and stabilized [68]. The pH of leachate for the electro-oxidation experiments is varied for pH values with adequate 5 N H2SO4 or 5 N of NaOH 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 & 7 at the conditions of 1 g/L of sodium persulfate, 0.35 g/L of M-GQDs, 4.5 V, 3 L/min aerations and a 2 cm electrode distance. The corresponding removal percentage of UV254 is found to be 98 %, 99 %, 83 %, 75 %, 44 %, & 5 % and TOC is found to be 84 %, 84 %, 56 %, 44 %, 26 %, & 0 % as shown in Fig. 6(a). The eq. (3) below is used to calculate the removal efficiency of UV254, COD, TOC, DOC, BOD, SUVA, and NH3-N [52,69].
(3)
Removal efficiency
%
=
Initial concentration − Final concentration
Initial concentration
X
100
The optimal removal percentage of both TOC and UV254 is found at a pH of 3 and the removal percentage decreases when the pH is elevated to alkaline conditions. The raw leachate contains 6470 mg/L of chlorides, which could lead to the generation of hypochlorous acid at acidic conditions and hypochlorite ions ClO- at basic conditions for the degradation of organics. The hypochlorous acid generates HClO- ions (E0 = 1.49 V), a stronger oxidation agent than the ClO- (E0 = 0.89 V) ions [70]. It has been previously reported that the humic acid degradation at the electrodes is higher in acidic conditions than in alkaline ones. The OH• radicals generated during the electro-oxidation have a shorter lifetime in basic conditions than in acidic conditions [66]. The formation of sulfate radicals (SO4 −) which have an oxidation potential greater than OH• radicals, tends to reduce with the increase in pH [71]. Owing to the combination of all these factors, it can be proposed that there is a decrease in the removal percentage of TOC and UV254 with an increase in pH.

By adjusting its dose to 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 g/L, the impact of persulfate addition on the leachate degradation of pollutants is examined under the conditions of pH of 3, 0.35 g/L of M-GQDs, 4.5 V, 3 L/min aerations and a 2 cm electrode distance. The corresponding UV254 removal percentages are 53 %, 84 %, 99 %, 83 % & 81 % and the TOC removal percentages are found to be 35 %, 78 %, 84 %, 78 % & 67 % as given in Fig. 6(b). In the absence of persulfate, the removal efficacy of TOC and UV254 is significantly diminished. The introduction of persulfate leads to the formation of sulfate radicals, which possess a higher oxidation potential than OH•, hence facilitating the breakdown of contaminants [8,71]. The observed phenomena indicate that the removal efficiency improves as the dose rises from 0 g/L to 1 g/L. However, it is noted that additional increases in dosage result in a reduction in the removal rate. This phenomenon could potentially be attributed to the generation of more ions, which act as scavengers for S2O8 2− and SO4 −. The mechanism is given by the equations below [72].

The quenching agents namely ethanol for quenching both OH• & SO4 •- radicals and tertiary butyl alcohol for quenching OH• radicals alone are added to study the role of OH• and SO4 •- radicals in the electro-oxidation reaction at the optimum conditions [73]. When ethanol alone is added the removal efficiency of TOC and UV254 has been reduced to 26 % & 35 %. Simultaneously, when tertiary butyl alcohol is added the removal efficiency of TOC and UV254 decreased to 44 % & 53 % as shown in Fig. 7(f). This study shows that the SO4 •- radicals play a major role than OH• radicals in the degradation of pollutants [52].

To explore how voltage affects the electrochemical response, it has been varied for different voltages, viz., 1.5, 3, 4.5, 6, and 7.5 V, at a condition of pH of 3, 1 g/L of sodium persulfate, 0.35 g/L of M-GQDs, 3 L/min aeration and a 2 cm electrode distance. The corresponding percentages of UV254 and TOC elimination are respectively 82 %, 85 %, 99 %, 82 % & 82 % and 48 %, 54 %, 84 %, 76 %, & 69 %. The findings are presented in Fig. 6(c). An appreciable increase in the removal percentages of UV254 and TOC is seen upon increasing the voltage from 1.5 V to 4.5 V. In simpler terms, an increase in current density causes a slight rise in the oxidation rate because it sends more charge to the cell, which contributes to the production of more OH• [74]. Direct and indirect electro-oxidation are the two types of electrochemical oxidation reactions. In direct oxidation, contaminants are adsorbed to the anode surface and degraded through an anodic electron transfer reaction. This phenomenon can occur in single or multiple stages, and it persists until the final oxidation product, which is primarily CO2, is produced [75]. It takes place at a lower current density, before O2 evolution, at a delayed reaction rate, and is dependent on the anode's electrocatalytic activity. Strong oxidants such as hydroxyl radicals and active chlorine species originate close to the electrode surface and are then dispersed into the bulk solution to oxidize the contaminants via a variety of pathways during indirect oxidation. The production of active chlorine (hypochlorous acid, gaseous chlorine, or hypochlorite) by ions of chloride in landfill leachate by indirect oxidation at the anode surface aids in the removal of TOC [76].

Nevertheless, beyond a voltage threshold of 4.5 V, the rate of removal percentage does not exhibit a substantial increase but rather experiences a minor decline. This decrease in efficiency ultimately increases energy consumption. This is mostly due to the wastage of significant quantities of OH• in non-oxidative processes, such as the evolution of oxygen, which disrupts the overall efficacy of the process [77]. When excess voltage occurs, several unwanted reactions occur, resulting in a reduction in the removal percentage [78,79].

M-GQDs provide the active sites for the persulfate stimulation. The impact of the dosage level of M-GQDs as catalysts on the degradation of leachate pollutants has been investigated. The M-GQDs dosage has been varied for 0, 0.175, 0.35, & 0.7 g/L under the conditions of pH of 3, 1 g/L of sodium persulfate, 4.5 V, 3 L/min aeration, and a 2 cm electrode distance. The corresponding removal percentages of TOC are determined to be 53 %, 67 %, 84 % & 83 % whereas the removal percentages of UV254 are found to be 62 %, 79 %, 74 %, 99 % & 98 % as shown in Fig. 6(d). The percentage elimination of TOC and UV254 is quite low when M-GQDs are not used as catalysts. This demonstrates unequivocally that the catalyst is crucial for the activation of persulfate and the decomposition of leachate contaminants. The amount of TOC and UV254 removal increases with the addition of the catalyst, reaching its maximum removal percentage at a dosage of 0.35 g/L for the M-GQDs. From the BET analysis, it is obvious that the M-GQDs have a good surface area and pore diameter similar to previous literature comprising the nature of good catalysis [19]. The quantum dots play the catalyst role due to electrostatic attraction, porous nature, adsorption on the surface, delocalized π electrons, functional & heteroatoms group attachment, edge defects, etc., represented in Fig. 2. The general overall mechanism of sulfate radicals generation by any carbon catalysts is given below [12].

The removal of TOC and UV254 marginally reduces as catalyst concentration rises to 0.7 g/L. This may be caused by the increase in active sites when the catalyst concentration rises, which causes M-GQDs to clump together on the persulfate's active sites thereby decreasing the process's effectiveness [71,80]. The comparative table with the present work for removing various pollutants with sulfate oxidation and carbon catalyst is represented in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of distance between the electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The spacing between the electrodes changed for 2, 3, 4, and 5 cm at the conditions of pH of 3, 1 g/L of sodium persulfate, 0.35 g/L of M-GQDs, 3 L/min aerations, and 4.5 V to check its influence on the degradation behavior. The corresponding percentages of UV254 and TOC elimination are 99 %, 94 %, 90 % & 89 % and 84 %, 80 %, 78 % & 77 % respectively. Fig. 6(e) depicts the research results. When the spacing between electrodes is decreased, the resistance of the cell lowers, and the flow of current increases, according to the principles outlined in Ohm's law [90]. Reducing the distance between electrodes might alleviate mass transfer constraints linked to the migration of contaminants toward the surface of the electrode, hence expediting their electrochemical conversion [91,92].

The aeration contributes significantly to the efficiency of the electro-oxidation process. It is generally provided in the cathode vicinity because the generation of H2O2 mainly depends on the cathode. Aeration can potentially alleviate the development of a passive layer often observed on the cathode surface, enhancing anodic reactions, and facilitating the improved generation of radicals within the solution. Furthermore, the aeration reduces the settleability of salts and catalysts at the bottom by increasing circulation and enhancing removal efficiency [93]. It also helps in the transport of pollutants toward the electrodes. Two speeds of aeration, 3, and 1.5 L/min, are given near the cathode vicinity at the optimized conditions of pH of 3, 0.35 g/L M-GQDs, 1 g/L PS, 4.5 V, and a 2 cm inter-electrode distance. The TOC and UV254 removal are found to be 79 % and 84 % at 1.5 L/min aeration, as well as 85 % and 99 % at 3 L/min as shown in Fig. 6(f). When no aeration is given, the removal percentages of TOC and UV254 are 44 % and 62 %, respectively. This again substantiates the crucial role of aeration in the electro-oxidation process [94]. Thus, at all the optimized conditions of pH of 3, 1 g/L of sodium persulfate, 0.35 g/L M-GQDs, 4.5 V, 2 cm electrode distance, and 3 L/min aeration, the removal percentage of other parameters like COD, BOD, DOC, SUVA, and NH3-N, is found to be 97 %, 45 %, 81 %, 63 %, and 90 %. The biodegradability index BOD/COD ratio has elevated from 0.040 to 0.656 confirming the degradation of organic compounds. (Table ST1).

Table ST3 contains the findings of the analysis of heavy metals. The decrease in Al, Ca, Cr, Cu, & Fe could be attributed to the hydroxide production with the electro-oxidation reaction [95]. The metal hydroxides are larger particles with a higher density than the water that form precipitates and settles down. The carcinogen chromium has not leached from the electrode after 180 min of reaction, confirming the stainless steel's favorable stability for the electro-oxidation reaction.

Cost analysis primarily establishes a technology's suitability for selection as a substitute for already-existing technology. The cost of a whole treatment will depend largely on the energy consumption. Energy consumption (E) per kilogram of COD in kWh/kg of COD is evaluated using the equation below (22).
(22)
E =
1000 UIT
C V
where U is the voltage, T is the time of reaction in hours (h), I is the current in amperes (A), V is the leachate volume in the reactor in cubic meters (l) and C is the COD in mg/L [97,101]. Energy usage for this electro-oxidation leachate treatment is estimated to be 0.44 kWh/kg COD under optimal conditions. Table 2 shows the comparison of different electro-oxidation for leachate treatment for energy consumption and COD removal efficiency with the present work.

The persulfate-aided M-GQDs catalytic electro-oxidation is regarded as being a first-order process since the UV254 and TOC concentrations decline progressively with the rise in duration during the first 180 min is estimated using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic eq. (23) as shown below [69,102].
(23)
ln
Co C
= kt
where t is the time, k is the rate constant, Co is the beginning concentration, and C is the final concentration at time t. The rate constant is found to be k1 = 0.018 L mol−1 S−1 for UV254 removal, and the rate constant is found to be k2 = 0.010 L mol−1 S−1 calculated using the equation mentioned above. The lower rate constant shows that the reaction occurs at a slower rate. The kinetics fit well with pseudo-first-order reactions with linear regression R2 = 0.99 for UV254 and R2 = 0.94 for TOC removal, as shown in Fig. S4(a). The UV–Vis analysis of untreated and treated leachate samples at the optimum circumstances is shown in Fig. S4(b). The curve becomes flat from raw to treated sample with an increase in time revealing the degradation of organic compounds.

The 3D EEM analysis to ensure the presence of dissolved organic matter (DOM) in raw and treated leachate at 60 min, 120 min, and 180 min has been shown in Fig. 7. The DOM has been classified into five regions, as shown in Table ST4 [103]. The dense fluorescence intensity is discovered in all the regions of the untreated leachate sample, as shown in Fig. 7(a), except for region I. The largest contour coverage area is found in region V, indicating the existence of more humic acid-like compounds. Medium contour area coverage is found in regions II, III & IV, showing the medium-level presence of hydrophobic acid, aromatic protein II, fulvic acid-like compounds, compounds like proteins, and soluble microbial by-product-like compounds. There is no contour area in region I, denoting the absence of aromatic protein I. Since region I is not present in the untreated leachate, it is not identified in the after-treatment analysis. The 60-min electro-oxidated leachate at the optimum conditions of pH of 3, 1 g/L persulfate dosage, 0.35 g/L M-GQDs, 4.5 V, 2 cm electrode spacing, and 3 L/min aerations as shown in Fig. 7(b), contains a considerable reduction in the intensity and contour area of regions II, III, IV & V. The contour area gradually decreases in 120 min as shown in Fig. 7(c). It fully vanishes at 180 min as shown in Fig. 7(d) and no intensity is detected due to any byproducts after 240 min Fig. 7(e). This shows that the persulfate with GQDs catalysis combined with electro-oxidation well acting in the degradation of dissolved organic matter.

A GC–MS test is carried out to ensure the type of leachate compounds and the by-products generated during the degradation process. 11 compounds are detected in the raw leachate, 24 compounds in 60 min, 28 compounds in 120 min, and 20 compounds after 180 min of degradation. A few compound structures of raw and treated leachate are given in Tables ST5 and ST6. The chromatogram graph is given in Fig. S5(a-d). The increase in the number of compounds at 60 min and 120 min shows the degradation occurs with more by-product formation. The observed reduction in the number of compounds at the 180-min mark suggests a decrease in the generation of by-products, indicating a slower rate of reaction or the approach toward the completion of the reaction. The toxic nature of compounds in raw leachate has been discussed as follows according to the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) [104]. Benzaldehyde, 2,4-dimethyl is detected (tr = 11.296), which is acutely toxic. Hexadecane (tr = 15.014) is fatal if swallowed. Benzoic acid, 4-ethoxy-, ethyl ester (tr = 15.443) is detected which causes skin as well as eye irritation and is harmful if swallowed. Octadecane, 1-iodo- (tr = 15.562) is detected which causes skin, eye, and respiratory irritation if it comes in contact. Phenol, 2,4-di-t-butyl-6-nitro- (tr = 17.407) is detected which is acutely toxic when swallowed, causes serious eye irritation, and causes long-lasting harmful effects to aquatic life. n-hexadecanoic acid (tr = 22.865) and octadecanoic acid (tr = 29.799) are detected which are strong eyes, skin, and respiratory irritants as well as detrimental to aquatic life with long-term consequences. 1,3-Benzenedicarboxylic acid, bis (2-ethylhexyl) is the most dangerous compound detected (tr = 34.301) and may cause fertility damage and be toxic to the aquatic environment. The following section discusses the toxicity of byproducts created at 60 min. The compounds pentacosane (tr = 12.171), octadecane (tr = 14.583), hexadecane (tr = 14.938), and eicosane (tr = 15.018) are observed to be present and it is known to have lethal effects when ingested or inhaled into the respiratory system. The compound octadecane, 1-iodo- (tr = 14.912), benzoic acid, 4-ethoxy-, ethyl ester (tr = 15.442), 7,9-Di-tert-butyl-1-oxaspiro (4,5) deca-6,9-diene-2,8-dione (tr = 21.916) are observed to elicit skin, ocular and respiratory irritation upon direct contact. On extended exposure, the compounds 2,4-Di-tert-butylphenol (tr = 15.232), tetradecanoic acid (tr = 18.424), n-hexadecanoic acid (tr = 22.853), octadecanoic acid (tr = 26.78) produces organ toxicity as well as eye, skin, and respiratory irritation. When exposed, it is also extremely harmful to aquatic life. Hexadecanoic acid, methyl ester (tr = 22.01) has allergenic properties upon skin exposure. 1,3-benzenedicarboxylic acid, bis(2-ethylhexyl) present in the raw sample is also detected after 60 min of treatment.

The following section discusses the toxicity of byproducts created at 120 min. A compound like dodecane (tr = 12.809) has been identified and there is extensive documentation regarding its potential to inflict significant harm if ingested or inhaled into the respiratory system. The compounds namely 1-iodo-octadecane (tr = 14.078), benzoic acid, 4-ethoxy-, ethyl ester (tr = 15.446), docosane (tr = 15.56), tetradecanoic acid (tr = 18.419), 7,9-Di-tert-butyl-1-oxaspiro (4,5) deca-6,9-diene-2,8-dione (tr = 21.912) has been identified as a substance that can induce irritation of the skin, eyes, and respiratory system upon direct contact. The compound 2,4-Di-tert-butylphenol has been found to exhibit organ toxicity and induce irritation in the eyes, skin, and respiratory system. When subjected to exposure, it poses a significant threat to the well-being of aquatic organisms. Hexadecane (tr = 14.937), eicosane (tr = 15.018), hexadecanoic acid, methyl ester (tr = 22.009), n-hexadecanoic acid (tr = 22.846), octadecanoic acid (tr = 26.774) and 1,3-benzenedicarboxylic acid, bis(2-ethylhexyl) (tr = 34.3) are also detected at 120 min.

The ensuing segment delves into the harmful nature of byproducts generated at the 180 min mark. Octadecane, 1-iodo- (tr = 12.171), eicosane (tr = 15.018), tetradecanoic acid (tr = 18.447), n-hexadecanoic acid (tr = 22.864), are detected after 180 min. 1-Heptadecene (tr = 17.385), which is deadly if consumed and penetrates the airways, is found and produces skin irritation upon touch. Contact with 7,9-di-tert-butyl-1-oxaspiro (4,5) deca-6,9-diene-2,8-dione (tr = 21.914) irritates the skin, eyes, and lungs. Octadecanoic acid (tr = 26.779), 2-Amino-1-phenylethanol, TMS derivative (tr = 29.498) has been observed to induce respiratory, ocular, and skin irritation upon contact, as well as pose a threat to aquatic life when exposed.

The overall number of toxic compounds from raw to 60 min & 120 min increases and decreases at 180 min. The generated by-products are also toxic. The number of poisonous compounds in the leachate at different toxicity levels is given in Table ST7 classified according to NCBI. No fertility-damaging compounds have been detected after 180 min of treatment. The presence of fatal lethal compounds increases at 60 min and only one compound is found after 180 min of treatment which also has an overall curve area of compound <2 %. The number of acute toxic compounds decreases and at 180 min of treatment, 2 compounds are present, with an overall curve area of <2 %. The skin, eye, and respiratory irritation also decreases and the detected compounds after 180 min have an overall GC–MS curve area of <2 %. Overall, we can conclude that the compound's toxicity reduces after 180 min of treatment. The O/C to H/C proportion of untreated and treated compounds detected by GC–MS is given in Fig. S6(a) to (d). The CHON (carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen) elemental composition and molecular weight distribution detected from the GC–MS compounds are given in Fig. S6(e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical, and morphological features of S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sludge generated by the electro-oxidation study is turned into quantum dots, and their various characteristics are discussed as follows. Fig. 8(a) and S7(a, b) depict the morphological and aggregation characteristics of S-CQDs acquired using HRTEM. The S-CQDs are circular and irregularly arranged. The Gaussian fit pattern in Fig. S7(c) reveals that S-CQDs have a uniform distribution and a mean particle size of 2.35 nm. The SAED configuration Fig. 8(b) confirms the amorphous state of the S-CQDs. The S-CQDs, like M-GQDs, emit green fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet light. The S-CQDs' XRD pattern is seen in Fig. 8(c). Peak diffraction values at 230 imply an amorphous carbon nature. Peak diffraction values at 230 imply an amorphous carbon nature. The (002) peak (JCPDS Card No., 75–0444) is responsible for the peak that S-CQDs. The FT-IR spectrum is seen in Fig. 8(d). The circular band at 3340 cm−1 indicates OH stretching [46]. The peak at 1550 cm−1 represents an alkene's CC stretching or CO vibration of the carboxylic acid or amide group stretching. The peak at 1370 cm−1 corresponds to the NO stretch, indicating the nitrates' functional group existence [38]. The band at 602 cm−1 is due to CS stretching vibration, which indicates the presence of a hydrogen sulfide functional group [51]. The mere existence of such functional groups suggests that the produced S-CQDs are hydrophilic. Fig. 8(e) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. A wide absorbance peak at 310 nm indicates the occurrence of migration of aromatic polycyclic CO bonds in dots [54] The band gap's energy is determined to be 4 eV, the same as in M-GQDs. The bandwidth illustrates the suitability of the produced S-CQDs for higher voltage component applications. Fig. 8(f) displays the PL emission spectra at excitation wavelengths spanning 280 to 440 nm in 20-nm intervals. When the excitation wavelength exceeded 380 nm, the emitted intensities decreased and displayed a redshift, demonstrating the presence of heteroatoms with different emissive areas in quantum dots [105]. The quantum yield is computed at 6.73 % based on the photoluminescence and UV–Vis readings. This yield is more than the quantum yield of the green production of quantum dots derived from organic plants, fruits, etc., [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of quantum dots for anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 shows the usage of M-GQDs and S-CQDs for thumbprint generation on ordinary paper, without and with UV–Vis's radiation fluorescence, using digital image capture. The fingerprint step-by-step development procedure is given in Fig. 9(a). Fingerprint development takes place on white paper, as shown in Fig. 9(b, f), and fingerprint imprinting with M-GQDs and S-CQDs brown powder, as specified in the white paper, is shown in Fig. 9(c, g). The capacity to create fingerprints without changing the latent shape of the fingerprint is crucial for anti-counterfeiting applications. The quality is frequently tested in three ways [34]. The first method preserves the overall ridge flow pattern, resembling the original fingerprint. The second way is to employ identifiable points or min details, such as ridges (1), hook (2), dots (3), and bifurcations (4), which correspond to three precise locations with points of significance under UV, and normal light as shown in Fig. 9(d1-d4), (h1-h4), (e1-e4) and (i1-i4). The consistent boundary arrangement is an additional quality check, and it is fairly comparable to the brown powder and ultraviolet light fingerprint. As a result, M-GQDs and S-CQDs powder fingerprint generation meet the quality standards for traditional fingerprint generation in all appreciates. The synthesized M-GQDs and S-CQDs liquid are used as paint without solvents or other components. A word is written on a typical white paper, using M-GQDs and S-CQDs liquid as shown in Fig. 9(j, n) in natural light and Fig. 9(k, o) in UV light. The appealing fluorescence shows that the created quantum dots are appropriate for fluorescent art applications. Fluorescence painting samples are packaged in airtight packaging and stored for three months. After three months, the fluorescence of the dots is examined for persistence under ultraviolet light. It turns out that the luminescent painting retains the same luminescence after three months demonstrating that the dots are appropriate for the application. This is seen in Fig. 9(l, p). The PL intensity ratio PL/PLo for synthesized quantum dots stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C, at ambient temperature, and light is given in Fig. 9(m, q). P0 denotes the initial PL, whereas P represents the PL after 90 days. The graph in Fig. 9(m, q) demonstrates that the PL is essentially constant under ideal storage conditions and somewhat lower under normal room conditions.

In this study, the persulfate is activated by green synthesized M-GQDs to degrade matured leachate in electro-oxidation with scrap stainless steel electrodes. At the optimized conditions, the significant removal of DOM, TOC, COD, UV254, and NH3-N is found with less energy consumption. The GC–MS analysis results attune to the reduction of micropollutants, and the by-products formed are less toxic than the parental compounds. The sludge generated in the electro-oxidation treatment is used to formulate the S-CQDs. The M-GQDs and S-CQDs give stable fluorescence and are successfully implied for anti-counterfeiting. Thus, the waste is converted into a catalyst for wastewater treatment, and the simultaneous sludge generated is also converted into a useful product, leading the way to a sustainable environment. Both the defatted Moringa seeds and the sludge-extracted quantum dots are profoundly suitable for a variety of future energy conversion and storage applications.
Not applicable.
The material presented in this manuscript has not been previously published and is not simultaneously under consideration by any other journal.
No funding has been received to carry out the research work.
